ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for croissant sandwich with hashbrown; mapped to nearest parent class 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Croissant-based sandwich — composite breakfast item with processed meat, egg, and fried potato side
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Croissants) used as the primary anchor for the croissant component; composite meal nature acknowledged. No single FoodEx2 code covers the full set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite breakfast set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g, comprising one ham and egg croissant sandwich (approximately 140–150 g) and one hashbrown patty (approximately 50–60 g), as commonly served at Singapore café and fast-food outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Ham and Egg Croissant with Hashbrown — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record for 'Ham and Egg Croissant with Hashbrown' (cr_id F08020036). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported in HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (croissant + fried egg + ham + hashbrown) and documented as approximate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for croissant and fried potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries: plain croissant GI ~67 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); deep-fried potato/hashbrown GI ~75–80 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted composite estimate using approximate carbohydrate contribution of croissant (~60%) and hashbrown (~40%) yields blended GI ≈ 72. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (72 × 31.7 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.8; adjusted to 18.1 per 200 g serving accounting for fibre offset. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (440 mg/100 g) with critically low potassium (17 mg/100 g) and minimal dietary fibre (1.72 g/100 g), creating an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal due to its high sodium content (440 mg/100 g) from processed ham and croissant, compounded by negligible potassium (17 mg/100 g) which provides no meaningful blood-pressure offset; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 880 mg sodium, approaching 40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from refined croissant pastry and deep-fried hashbrown, combined with low dietary fibre (1.72 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal due to its high GI (~72) and high glycemic load (~18 per serving), driven by refined croissant flour and deep-fried potato; the low fibre content (1.72 g/100 g) provides insufficient glycaemic buffering, and estimated trans fat from croissant pastry further impairs insulin sensitivity — substituting with a wholemeal bread sandwich and baked potato is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (25.51 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from croissant pastry and deep-frying, alongside high sodium (440 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (11.01 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal due to its high saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) content from the buttery croissant and deep-fried hashbrown, which raise LDL cholesterol and promote arterial inflammation; the high sodium (440 mg/100 g) further elevates cardiovascular risk, and a 200 g serving provides approximately 51 g total fat, exceeding the recommended daily saturated fat intake for at-risk individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation (AGE formation), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from croissant pastry, and arachidonic acid from processed pork ham
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal as deep-frying of the hashbrown generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation, the estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) in the croissant pastry is strongly pro-inflammatory, and the nightshade-derived potato in the hashbrown may exacerbate solanine-related joint sensitivity in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham and Egg Croissant with Hashbrown is a Western-style breakfast set meal commonly served at Singapore cafés, fast-food outlets, and school canteens. The dish consists of a buttery, flaky croissant sandwich filled with sliced picnic ham and a fried egg, accompanied by a deep-fried hashbrown patty on the side. The croissant provides a rich, layered, slightly sweet pastry base with a crisp exterior and soft interior; the fried egg adds a savoury, protein-rich element; the picnic ham contributes a salty, processed-meat flavour; and the hashbrown delivers a crispy, starchy, deep-fried potato component. Nutritionally, this meal is energy-dense at 354 kcal per 100 g, with high total fat (25.51 g/100 g) including estimated saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from the croissant pastry and frying oil. Sodium is high at 440 mg/100 g, potassium is critically low at 17 mg/100 g, and dietary fibre is minimal at 1.72 g/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), making this meal unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The meal is not halal-certified due to the pork ham component. It is a popular breakfast and working-lunch option in Singapore's Western food stall and café culture, reflecting the localised adoption of Western fast-food formats.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020036) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for croissant and deep-fried potato with documented weighting methodology. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.8 g/100 g) is an approximate estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (croissant + fried egg + ham + hashbrown) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat for this record. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on typical croissant pastry composition and deep-frying oil; not directly measured in the HPB source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this exact composite meal. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this record. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond those reported in HPB Foodo (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) not available in the primary source for this composite set meal as of 2026. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. Meal is not halal due to pork ham component. 8. Cholesterol value of 11.01 mg/100 g from HPB source appears low for a fried-egg-containing meal; this value is preserved as reported from the approved source without modification.
